abstract:
Effective information searching is essential for enhancing the reasoning and
generation capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Recent research has
explored using reinforcement learning (RL) to improve LLMs' search capabilities
by interacting with live search engines in real-world environments. While these
approaches show promising results, they face two major challenges: (1)
Uncontrolled Document Quality: The quality of documents returned by search
engines is often unpredictable, introducing noise and instability into the
training process. (2) Prohibitively High API Costs: RL training requires
frequent rollouts, potentially involving hundreds of thousands of search
requests, which incur substantial API expenses and severely constrain
scalability. To address these challenges, we introduce ZeroSearch, a novel RL
framework that incentivizes the capabilities of LLMs to use a real search
engine with simulated searches during training. Our approach begins with
lightweight supervised fine-tuning to transform the LLM into a retrieval module
capable of generating both useful and noisy documents in response to a query.
During RL training, we employ a curriculum-based rollout strategy that
incrementally degrades the quality of generated documents, progressively
eliciting the model's reasoning ability by exposing it to increasingly
challenging retrieval scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that
ZeroSearch effectively incentivizes the search capabilities of LLMs using a 3B
LLM as the retrieval module. Remarkably, a 7B retrieval module achieves
comparable performance to the real search engine, while a 14B retrieval module
even surpasses it. Furthermore, it generalizes well across both base and
instruction-tuned models of various parameter sizes and is compatible with a
wide range of RL algorithms.

keyInsights: Large language models (LLMs) can effectively simulate real-world search engines, providing a controllable and cost-efficient environment for training search-augmented LLMs.
Training LLMs with a simulated search environment yields superior performance compared to using real search engines, largely due to enhanced training stability and controlled document quality.
A curriculum learning strategy that gradually increases the proportion of noisy documents during training improves the robustness and reasoning capabilities of search-augmented LLMs.
originalProblem: Training large language models (LLMs) to effectively use real-world search engines via reinforcement learning (RL) incurs prohibitively high API costs.
Real search engines provide uncontrolled document quality, which can lead to unstable and inefficient RL training for search-augmented LLMs.
LLMs inherently have static knowledge, leading to issues like hallucination and an inability to access real-time information without external tools.
results: ZEROSEARCH consistently outperforms all baseline methods, including models trained with real search engines like Search-R1, achieving higher average Exact Match (EM) scores across diverse question answering datasets.

The framework significantly reduces training costs, cutting expenses by over 85% compared to using commercial search APIs (e.g., ~$70.8 vs. ~$586.7 for 64,000 search requests over 12 hours of training).

It demonstrates strong generalizability across different LLM sizes (3B to 7B) and model families, with performance improving as the simulation LLM size increases, and benefits from curriculum rollout for enhanced stability.
solution: Introduces an RL framework that replaces real search engine interactions with a lightweight fine-tuned LLM acting as a 'simulation search engine' during training.

Employs a curriculum learning-based rollout mechanism that dynamically controls the probability of generating 'noisy' documents, progressing from easier (more useful) to harder (more noisy) training scenarios.
Utilizes a rule-based F1 score for reward calculation and applies loss masking for document tokens to ensure stable and effective reinforcement learning.
paper_summary.summary: A framework called ZEROSEARCH enables training large language models (LLMs) to leverage search capabilities by replacing costly real-world search engine APIs with a simulated LLM environment. This approach from Alibaba Group's Tongyi Lab achieves superior performance while substantially reducing training costs.

title: ZeroSearch: Incentivize the Search Capability of LLMs without Searching

abstract:
Existing Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have shown the potential of
reinforcement learning (RL) to enhance the complex reasoning capabilities of
Large Language Models~(LLMs). While they achieve remarkable performance on
challenging tasks such as mathematics and coding, they often rely on their
internal knowledge to solve problems, which can be inadequate for
time-sensitive or knowledge-intensive questions, leading to inaccuracies and
hallucinations. To address this, we propose \textbf{R1-Searcher}, a novel
two-stage outcome-based RL approach designed to enhance the search capabilities
of LLMs. This method allows LLMs to autonomously invoke external search systems
to access additional knowledge during the reasoning process. Our framework
relies exclusively on RL, without requiring process rewards or distillation for
a cold start. % effectively generalizing to out-of-domain datasets and
supporting both Base and Instruct models. Our experiments demonstrate that our
method significantly outperforms previous strong RAG methods, even when
compared to the closed-source GPT-4o-mini.

keyInsights: Reinforcement Learning (RL) consistently outperforms Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) in teaching LLMs genuine search capabilities, leading to more accurate query timing and relevance.
A two-stage reward design, using F1-score for answer quality and strict format rewards, is crucial for stable RL training and preventing 'reward hacking'.
Training with a curriculum that includes 'difficult' questions and diverse datasets significantly enhances the model's overall performance and generalization abilities for retrieval and reasoning.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) primarily rely on internal knowledge, leading to hallucinations, outdated information, and inability to access private or real-time data.

Existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods face limitations such as reliance on complex prompt engineering, memorization issues with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) distillation, and high inference overhead from test-time scaling methods.
Current RAG solutions often struggle with generalization to out-of-domain datasets and adapting to different or online retrieval environments.
results: R1-Searcher significantly outperforms strong RAG baselines, including GPT-4o-mini based methods, achieving up to 48.22% higher performance on HotpotQA and 21.72% on 2WikiMultiHopQA (LLM-as-Judge metric).
The model demonstrates robust generalization, performing well on out-of-domain datasets like Musique and Bamboogle despite being trained on a limited dataset.
R1-Searcher effectively generalizes to online search scenarios, showing an 18.2% improvement when switching from local retrieval to Google Web Search API on the Bamboogle dataset.
solution: R1-Searcher proposes a novel two-stage outcome-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework that enables LLMs to autonomously invoke and utilize external search systems.

The approach modifies the Reinforce++ algorithm with RAG-based rollouts for integrating retrieved documents and a retrieval mask for loss calculation, ensuring the model focuses on generating queries and answers.

It uses purely outcome-based rewards, with Stage-1 incentivizing retrieval invocation and format adherence, and Stage-2 focusing on answer accuracy via F1-score and penalizing format errors, all without needing supervised fine-tuning for a cold start.
paper_summary.summary: R1-Searcher, from Renmin University of China, introduces a two-stage outcome-based reinforcement learning framework that enables Large Language Models to autonomously invoke and leverage external search systems. This approach significantly outperforms strong RAG baselines on multi-hop question answering benchmarks and demonstrates robust generalization to out-of-domain and online search scenarios.

title: R1-Searcher: Incentivizing the Search Capability in LLMs via  Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel at complex reasoning tasks but remain computationally expensive, limiting their practical deployment. To address this, recent works have focused on distilling reasoning capabilities into smaller language models (sLMs) using chain-of-thought (CoT) traces from teacher LLMs. However, this approach struggles in scenarios requiring rare factual knowledge or precise computation, where sLMs often hallucinate due to limited capability. In this work, we propose Agent Distillation, a framework for transferring not only reasoning capability but full task-solving behavior from LLM-based agents into sLMs with retrieval and code tools. We improve agent distillation along two complementary axes: (1) we introduce a prompting method called first-thought prefix to enhance the quality of teacher-generated trajectories; and (2) we propose a self-consistent action generation for improving test-time robustness of small agents. We evaluate our method on eight reasoning tasks across factual and mathematical domains, covering both in-domain and out-of-domain generalization. Our results show that sLMs as small as 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B parameters can achieve performance competitive with next-tier larger 1.5B, 3B, 7B models fine-tuned using CoT distillation, demonstrating the potential of agent distillation for building practical, tool-using small agents. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Distilling interactive, tool-augmented reasoning patterns rather than static reasoning traces allows sLMs to overcome their internal memory limitations and dynamically leverage external knowledge and computation.
The quality of teacher-generated trajectories is critical, and guiding the teacher's initial thought with a CoT-style prefix significantly improves the efficacy of the distilled agentic behavior.

Enhancing test-time robustness through techniques like sampling and consistency checks for actions (sag) is essential for small models to reliably interact with external tools and mitigate generation errors.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate advanced reasoning but their high computational cost limits practical deployment, especially in resource-constrained environments.
Traditional reasoning distillation (e.g., Chain-of-Thought) often leaves small language models (sLMs) struggling with rare factual knowledge or precise computations, leading to hallucinations.
A gap exists in effectively transferring dynamic, interactive agentic capabilities, which involve reasoning, acting with tools, and observing feedback, to much smaller, more efficient models.
results: Agent-distilled sLMs (e.g., 0.5B parameters) achieve performance competitive with or superior to next-tier larger CoT-distilled models (e.g., 1.5B parameters) across factual and mathematical reasoning tasks.
The approach yields improved generalization and factual accuracy on out-of-domain tasks, outperforming CoT-distilled models, including those augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

Both `first-thought prefix` and `self-consistent action generation` contribute to overall performance gains, with `sag` demonstrably reducing code parsing and execution errors, especially for smaller models.
solution: Introduced Agent Distillation, a framework that fine-tunes sLMs to imitate the full `reason-act-observe` trajectories of a teacher LLM agent using external retrieval and code execution tools.

Developed `first-thought prefix` (ftp) to guide the teacher agent's initial reasoning step, generating higher-quality trajectories for distillation by aligning with a robust CoT-style thought process.

Implemented `self-consistent action generation` (sag) during sLM inference, which samples multiple actions, filters invalid ones, and uses majority voting on observations to enhance robustness and ensure valid tool interactions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from KAIST, University of Wisconsin-Madison, and KRAFTON developed Agent Distillation, a framework that transfers advanced tool-using and interactive reasoning capabilities from large language model agents to small language models. This approach enables sLMs to achieve performance comparable to much larger models on complex factual and mathematical tasks.

title: Distilling LLM Agent into Small Models with Retrieval and Code Tools

abstract: We investigate the potential of large language models (LLMs) to serve as efficient simulators for agentic search tasks in reinforcement learning (RL), thereby reducing dependence on costly interactions with external search engines. To this end, we first quantify the intrinsic search capability of LLMs via structured prompting and repeated sampling, which we term Self-Search. Our results reveal that LLMs exhibit strong scaling behavior with respect to the inference budget, achieving high pass@k on question-answering benchmarks, including the challenging BrowseComp task. Building on these observations, we introduce Self-Search RL (SSRL), which enhances LLMs' Self-Search capability through format-based and rule-based rewards. SSRL enables models to iteratively refine their knowledge utilization internally, without requiring access to external tools. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that SSRL-trained policy models provide a cost-effective and stable environment for search-driven RL training, reducing reliance on external search engines and facilitating robust sim-to-real transfer. We draw the following conclusions: 1) LLMs possess world knowledge that can be effectively elicited to achieve high performance; 2) SSRL demonstrates the potential of leveraging internal knowledge to reduce hallucination; 3) SSRL-trained models integrate seamlessly with external search engines without additional effort. Our findings highlight the potential of LLMs to support more scalable RL agent training.

keyInsights: Large language models possess substantial intrinsic world knowledge that can be effectively elicited and scaled via repeated sampling, with smaller models sometimes rivaling larger ones with sufficient samples.
Instruction-tuned LLMs demonstrate a stronger ability to utilize their internal knowledge for self-search compared to base models, highlighting the impact of fine-tuning on knowledge elicitation.
The use of format-based rewards is critical for guiding LLMs to produce structured, multi-step reasoning trajectories that effectively integrate internal search capabilities.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) used as agents incur high costs and logistical complexity when frequently interacting with external search engines during reinforcement learning (RL) training.
Existing full-real and semi-real search methods for LLM agents require extensive rollouts and multi-turn API calls, leading to substantial computational and financial overhead.
Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches rely on static knowledge bases, lacking the dynamic and noisy characteristics of real-time web search.
results: SSRL achieves a 5.53x reduction in training time compared to simulated search baselines like ZeroSearch, while maintaining robust and steady reward growth.
SSRL-trained models consistently outperform baselines that utilize external or separately simulated search engines, as well as RAG-based methods, on various information-seeking QA benchmarks.

The approach demonstrates effective sim-to-real generalization, allowing SSRL-trained models to seamlessly integrate with real external search engines during inference, with an entropy-guided strategy reducing external search frequency by 20-42% without performance loss.
solution: SSRL introduces a "Full-Sim Search" paradigm where the LLM policy model functions as both the reasoning agent and the internal search engine, generating queries and information from its parametric knowledge.
The training methodology includes an information token mask to encourage comprehensive reasoning and a composite reward function (outcome + format rewards) to guide adherence to structured output.

An on-policy RL approach is used, where the policy model itself acts as the information provider, allowing the model to simultaneously enhance its reasoning and internal knowledge retrieval capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: Self-Search Reinforcement Learning (SSRL) allows large language models to act as internal search engines during training, significantly reducing the reliance on costly external search engines. This method improves training efficiency by 5.53x compared to prior approaches and demonstrates effective generalization to real-world search scenarios during inference.

title: SSRL: Self-Search Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a common strategy to reduce
hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs). While reinforcement learning
(RL) can enable LLMs to act as search agents by activating retrieval
capabilities, existing ones often underutilize their internal knowledge. This
can lead to redundant retrievals, potential harmful knowledge conflicts, and
increased inference latency. To address these limitations, an efficient and
adaptive search agent capable of discerning optimal retrieval timing and
synergistically integrating parametric (internal) and retrieved (external)
knowledge is in urgent need. This paper introduces the Reinforced
Internal-External Knowledge Synergistic Reasoning Agent (IKEA), which could
indentify its own knowledge boundary and prioritize the utilization of internal
knowledge, resorting to external search only when internal knowledge is deemed
insufficient. This is achieved using a novel knowledge-boundary aware reward
function and a knowledge-boundary aware training dataset. These are designed
for internal-external knowledge synergy oriented RL, incentivizing the model to
deliver accurate answers, minimize unnecessary retrievals, and encourage
appropriate external searches when its own knowledge is lacking. Evaluations
across multiple knowledge reasoning tasks demonstrate that IKEA significantly
outperforms baseline methods, reduces retrieval frequency significantly, and
exhibits robust generalization capabilities.

keyInsights: Models can be trained to effectively determine their own knowledge boundaries
Prioritizing internal knowledge when sufficient reduces retrieval redundancy and conflicts
Knowledge-seeking behavior learned through self-exploration generalizes well to new tasks
originalProblem: Current search agents over-rely on external knowledge retrieval even when LLMs have sufficient internal knowledge
Excessive retrievals introduce noise, knowledge conflicts, and increased latency
Lack of mechanisms for models to effectively determine when to use internal vs external knowledge
results: Significant reduction in retrieval frequency compared to baseline approaches
Maintained or improved performance across multiple knowledge-intensive reasoning benchmarks
Robust generalization capabilities demonstrated on out-of-distribution datasets
Ablation studies confirm importance of both reward design and balanced data construction
solution: IKEA agent framework that explicitly prompts models to assess their knowledge boundaries
Knowledge-boundary aware reward function that incentivizes appropriate use of internal vs external knowledge
Balanced training dataset construction with equal mix of internal/external knowledge-requiring questions
Reinforcement learning optimization using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)
paper_summary.summary: A reinforcement learning framework enables large language models to efficiently combine internal and external knowledge through adaptive search strategies, reducing retrieval frequency while maintaining or improving performance across multiple knowledge-intensive reasoning tasks through a novel knowledge-boundary aware reward function.

title: Reinforced Internal-External Knowledge Synergistic Reasoning for  Efficient Adaptive Search Agent

abstract: Augmenting large language models (LLMs) with external retrieval has become a standard method to address their inherent knowledge cutoff limitations. However, traditional retrieval-augmented generation methods employ static, pre-inference retrieval strategies, making them inadequate for complex tasks involving ambiguous, multi-step, or evolving information needs. Recent advances in test-time scaling techniques have demonstrated significant potential in enabling LLMs to dynamically interact with external tools, motivating the shift toward adaptive inference-time retrieval. Inspired by Information Foraging Theory (IFT), we propose InForage, a reinforcement learning framework that formalizes retrieval-augmented reasoning as a dynamic information-seeking process. Unlike existing approaches, InForage explicitly rewards intermediate retrieval quality, encouraging LLMs to iteratively gather and integrate information through adaptive search behaviors. To facilitate training, we construct a human-guided dataset capturing iterative search and reasoning trajectories for complex, real-world web tasks. Extensive evaluations across general question answering, multi-hop reasoning tasks, and a newly developed real-time web QA dataset demonstrate InForage's superior performance over baseline methods. These results highlight InForage's effectiveness in building robust, adaptive, and efficient reasoning agents.

keyInsights: Modeling search-enhanced reasoning as "information foraging" provides a robust, principled framework for LLMs to dynamically gather knowledge and adapt to complex information needs.
A multi-component reward system, valuing both final answer correctness and intermediate retrieval quality and efficiency, is essential for training LLMs in strategic information-seeking behavior.
Training on a fine-grained, human-guided dataset of iterative search trajectories is critical for equipping LLMs with the ability to perform complex, multi-hop reasoning.
originalProblem: Static Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is insufficient for complex, ambiguous, or multi-step information needs due to its pre-inference retrieval approach.
Current dynamic search-enhanced reasoning methods struggle with iteratively accumulating partial information patches and evaluating the context-dependent utility of retrieved data.
LLM optimization typically focuses only on final answer correctness, overlooking the effectiveness and efficiency of intermediate information retrieval steps.
results: InForage consistently surpassed all baselines, achieving an average Exact Match (EM) score of 41.0 across various QA benchmarks, demonstrating strong robustness and generalizability.

It showed superior performance on multi-hop tasks and a novel real-time web QA dataset, securing an EM of 44.1 on the "Self*" dataset, indicating its efficacy in complex, open-ended information-seeking.
Ablation studies confirmed that the custom dataset, supervised fine-tuning, and the specific information gain and efficiency penalty rewards are all crucial for the framework's superior performance.
solution: Develops InForage, an RL framework inspired by Information Foraging Theory, which models search-enhanced reasoning as an iterative process of gathering "information patches" guided by "information scent."
Implements a multi-component reward system that explicitly balances final answer accuracy (R_outcome), cumulative information gain (R_gain), and reasoning efficiency (R_efficiency).
Employs a two-stage training strategy: Supervised Fine-Tuning on a custom human-guided dataset for initial behavior learning, followed by Reinforcement Learning using PPO with the designed rewards.
paper_summary.summary: InForage, developed at Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence, is a reinforcement learning framework that optimizes large language models for search-enhanced, multi-step reasoning by emulating human information foraging strategies. The framework dynamically interacts with external knowledge, achieving an average Exact Match (EM) score of 41.0 across diverse question-answering benchmarks by explicitly rewarding intermediate retrieval quality and reasoning efficiency.

title: Scent of Knowledge: Optimizing Search-Enhanced Reasoning with Information Foraging

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel in complex reasoning tasks, and distilling their reasoning capabilities into smaller models has shown promise. However, we uncover an interesting phenomenon, which we term the Small Model Learnability Gap: small models ($\leq$3B parameters) do not consistently benefit from long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning or distillation from larger models. Instead, they perform better when fine-tuned on shorter, simpler reasoning chains that better align with their intrinsic learning capacity. To address this, we propose Mix Distillation, a simple yet effective strategy that balances reasoning complexity by combining long and short CoT examples or reasoning from both larger and smaller models. Our experiments demonstrate that Mix Distillation significantly improves small model reasoning performance compared to training on either data alone. These findings highlight the limitations of direct strong model distillation and underscore the importance of adapting reasoning complexity for effective reasoning capability transfer.
title: Small Models Struggle to Learn from Strong Reasoners

abstract: Reasoning-augmented search agents such as Search-R1, trained via reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), demonstrate remarkable capabilities in multi-step information retrieval from external knowledge sources. These agents address the limitations of their parametric memory by dynamically gathering relevant facts to address complex reasoning tasks. However, existing approaches suffer from a fundamental architectural limitation: they process search queries strictly sequentially, even when handling inherently parallelizable and logically independent comparisons. This sequential bottleneck significantly constrains computational efficiency, particularly for queries that require multiple entity comparisons. To address this critical limitation, we propose ParallelSearch, a novel reinforcement learning framework that empowers large language models (LLMs) to recognize parallelizable query structures and execute multiple search operations concurrently. Our approach introduces dedicated reward functions that incentivize the identification of independent query components while preserving answer accuracy through jointly considering correctness, query decomposition quality, and parallel execution benefits. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that ParallelSearch outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by an average performance gain of 2.9% across seven question-answering benchmarks. Notably, on parallelizable questions, our method achieves a 12.7% performance improvement while requiring only 69.6% of the LLM calls compared to sequential approaches.

keyInsights: Many complex queries can be naturally broken down into logically independent sub-components that can be executed concurrently without compromising reasoning quality or answer accuracy.

Explicitly designing a multi-component reinforcement learning reward function is crucial for teaching LLMs the complex behavior of intelligent query decomposition and efficient parallel search execution.

Optimizing the architectural interaction of LLMs with external tools for parallel processing, rather than solely scaling model size, offers a pathway to significant efficiency and performance improvements.
originalProblem: Existing reasoning-augmented search agents process all search queries sequentially, even when handling inquiries with inherently independent components.
This "sequential bottleneck" leads to significant computational overhead, increased latency, and prohibitive costs in deploying LLMs for complex, knowledge-intensive tasks.
Current reinforcement learning methods for LLMs primarily focus on answer correctness, lacking mechanisms to directly incentivize efficient, parallelized search strategies.
results: ParallelSearch achieves an average absolute Exact Match (EM) improvement of 2.9% across seven diverse question-answering benchmarks, with gains up to 12.7% on parallelizable question subsets.
The method significantly reduces the average number of LLM inference calls by 30.4%, requiring an average of 2.34 turns per question compared to 3.36 for sequential approaches.

The learned parallelization capabilities generalize well to out-of-domain datasets and surprisingly also show performance gains on purely sequential question subsets, indicating a more robust overall search strategy.
solution: A novel reinforcement learning framework, ParallelSearch, trains LLMs to identify parallelizable query structures and decompose them into multiple independent sub-queries in a single reasoning step.
The framework modifies the search-reasoning loop to execute these decomposed sub-queries concurrently via an external search engine, aggregating all results before returning to the LLM.

A comprehensive reward function, including outcome, decomposition, search count, and format rewards, explicitly incentivizes correct parallelization, search efficiency, and adherence to response structure.
paper_summary.summary: ParallelSearch enables Large Language Models to intelligently decompose complex queries into independent sub-queries for parallel execution via a novel reinforcement learning framework. This approach reduces LLM inference calls by 30.4% and achieves up to a 12.7% performance gain on parallelizable question-answering tasks, enhancing efficiency and accuracy.

title: ParallelSearch: Train your LLMs to Decompose Query and Search Sub-queries in Parallel with Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on multi-turn tool-integrated planning for knowledge-intensive and complex reasoning tasks. Existing implementations typically rely on a single agent, but they suffer from limited context length and noisy tool responses. A natural solution is to adopt a multi-agent framework with planner- and worker-agents to manage context. However, no existing methods support effective reinforcement learning post-training of tool-integrated multi-agent frameworks. To address this gap, we propose Multi-Agent Tool-Integrated Policy Optimization (MATPO), which enables distinct roles (planner and worker) to be trained within a single LLM instance using role-specific prompts via reinforcement learning. MATPO is derived from a principled credit assignment mechanism across planner and worker rollouts. This design eliminates the need to deploy multiple LLMs, which would be memory-intensive, while preserving the benefits of specialization. Experiments on GAIA-text, WebWalkerQA, and FRAMES show that MATPO consistently outperforms single-agent baselines by an average of 18.38% relative improvement in performance and exhibits greater robustness to noisy tool outputs. Our findings highlight the effectiveness of unifying multiple agent roles within a single LLM and provide practical insights for stable and efficient multi-agent RL training.

keyInsights: An LLM can effectively learn and perform distinct planner and worker roles when activated by role-specific prompts and exposed to diverse role experiences during RL training.
Implementing worker agent output summaries and recapping the original user query for worker agents significantly enhances multi-agent performance by improving context management and guidance.

Presenting worker outputs as "user messages" might implicitly bias the planner towards compliance, suggesting a need for alternative inter-agent communication formats to encourage more critical evaluation.
originalProblem: Single-agent tool-integrated LLMs struggle with context window saturation from verbose tool outputs and reasoning disruption from noisy tool responses.
Current multi-agent LLM systems primarily rely on inference-time prompt engineering, lacking effective reinforcement learning training methods to optimize collaborative behavior.
Training multi-agent systems with separate LLM models for each role introduces significant infrastructure complexity, memory consumption, and uneven workload distribution.
results: MATPO consistently outperformed single-agent GRPO, achieving an average relative performance improvement of 18.38% across GAIA-text, WebWalkerQA, and FRAMES datasets.
The method demonstrated enhanced robustness, showing more stable performance gains throughout training, unlike single-agent GRPO which often experienced drops.
Specific test accuracies achieved were 42.60% on GAIA-text, 33.00% on WebWalkerQA, and 63.64% on FRAMES using the Qwen3-14B-base model.
solution: MATPO extends Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to train multiple distinct agent roles (planner and worker) within a single LLM instance.
A novel credit assignment mechanism propagates the final verifiable reward from the planner's overall task completion to all LLM-generated tokens across both planner and nested worker rollouts.

The framework employs nested rollout functions and data batch augmentation to integrate experiences from both agent roles, with masking of system prompts and tool responses during policy gradient calculation.
paper_summary.summary: MiroMind AI introduces Multi-Agent Tool-Integrated Policy Optimization (MATPO), a reinforcement learning method that enables a single Large Language Model instance to perform multiple specialized agent roles for complex knowledge-intensive tasks. This approach achieves an average relative performance improvement of 18.38% over single-agent baselines across several benchmarks, demonstrating increased robustness and effective management of noisy tool outputs.

title: Multi-Agent Tool-Integrated Policy Optimization

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong capabilities as decision-making agents by interleaving reasoning and actions, as seen in ReAct-style frameworks. Yet, their practical deployment is constrained by high inference costs and large model sizes. We propose Structured Agent Distillation, a framework that compresses large LLM-based agents into smaller student models while preserving both reasoning fidelity and action consistency. Unlike standard token-level distillation, our method segments trajectories into {[REASON]} and {[ACT]} spans, applying segment-specific losses to align each component with the teacher's behavior. This structure-aware supervision enables compact agents to better replicate the teacher's decision process. Experiments on ALFWorld, HotPotQA-ReAct, and WebShop show that our approach consistently outperforms token-level and imitation learning baselines, achieving significant compression with minimal performance drop. Scaling and ablation results further highlight the importance of span-level alignment for efficient and deployable agents.
title: Structured Agent Distillation for Large Language Model

abstract: Large language models have demonstrated impressive reasoning capabilities but are inherently limited by their knowledge reservoir. Retrieval-augmented reasoning mitigates this limitation by allowing LLMs to query external resources, but existing methods often retrieve irrelevant or noisy information, hindering accurate reasoning. In this paper, we propose AutoRefine, a reinforcement learning post-training framework that adopts a new "search-and-refine-during-think" paradigm. AutoRefine introduces explicit knowledge refinement steps between successive search calls, enabling the model to iteratively filter, distill, and organize evidence before generating an answer. Furthermore, we incorporate tailored retrieval-specific rewards alongside answer correctness rewards using group relative policy optimization. Experiments on single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate that AutoRefine significantly outperforms existing approaches, particularly in complex, multi-hop reasoning scenarios. Detailed analysis shows that AutoRefine issues frequent, higher-quality searches and synthesizes evidence effectively.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Explicit knowledge refinement is crucial for effective context management and multi-hop knowledge linking, significantly reducing context length (from >600 to 100-200 tokens) while retaining essential information.
The tailored retrieval-specific reward component (`R_Ret`) effectively promotes better multi-turn search behavior and boosts the quality of the refinement process by approximately 20%.
AutoRefine's internal, RL-driven refinement strategy outperforms external summarization models, indicating that learned introspection and strategic planning for subsequent searches are vital.
originalProblem: Existing retrieval-augmented reasoning (RAR) methods often process full, noisy, or weakly relevant documents, making large language models (LLMs) susceptible to distractions, especially in multi-hop reasoning.

Current reinforcement learning (RL) based RAR methods primarily use outcome-based rewards, which provide insufficient direct guidance for improving the quality of the retrieval process itself or refining retrieved content.
results: Achieved an average accuracy gain of 6.9% (base variant) and 6.0% (instruct variant) over state-of-the-art baselines on seven diverse QA benchmarks.
Demonstrated particularly strong performance on multi-hop QA, with improvements of 8.3% on 2WikiMultihopQA and 4.5% on Musique, representing relative gains of 21% and 26.7% respectively.

Exhibited robust performance across varying retrieval depths, consistently boosting accuracy by 0.04 to 0.1, and maintained high search quality with a 10-15% higher search success rate on multi-hop benchmarks.
solution: Introduces a "search-and-refine-during-think" paradigm, extending LLM reasoning trajectories with an explicit `<refine>` action to filter, distill, and organize crucial evidence from retrieved documents.
Employs a multi-component, rule-based reward system that combines a standard outcome-based reward (R_Ans) with a novel retrieval-specific reward (R_Ret) to supervise the quality of refined knowledge.

Utilizes Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for training, where retrieved documents are masked out during loss computation to emphasize optimization of the model's thought processes, search queries, and refinement summaries.
paper_summary.summary: AutoRefine enhances large language models' retrieval-augmented reasoning by incorporating explicit knowledge refinement steps and retrieval-specific rewards within a reinforcement learning framework. This approach yields an average accuracy gain of 6.9% across seven diverse QA benchmarks, showing particular improvements in complex multi-hop reasoning tasks.

title: Search and Refine During Think: Facilitating Knowledge Refinement for Improved Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning

abstract: Recent deep research agents primarily improve performance by scaling reasoning depth, but this leads to high inference cost and latency in search-intensive scenarios. Moreover, generalization across heterogeneous research settings remains challenging. In this work, we propose \emph{Search More, Think Less} (SMTL), a framework for long-horizon agentic search that targets both efficiency and generalization. SMTL replaces sequential reasoning with parallel evidence acquisition, enabling efficient context management under constrained context budgets. To support generalization across task types, we further introduce a unified data synthesis pipeline that constructs search tasks spanning both deterministic question answering and open-ended research scenarios with task appropriate evaluation metrics. We train an end-to-end agent using supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, achieving strong and often state of the art performance across benchmarks including BrowseComp (48.6\%), GAIA (75.7\%), Xbench (82.0\%), and DeepResearch Bench (45.9\%). Compared to Mirothinker-v1.0, SMTL with maximum 100 interaction steps reduces the average number of reasoning steps on BrowseComp by 70.7\%, while improving accuracy.

keyInsights: The "Search More, Think Less" philosophy, prioritizing parallel evidence acquisition and information density over deep, linear sequential reasoning, leads to more efficient agent performance.
Search breadth, such as increasing the `top-k` parameter for web search results, serves as a more efficient scaling dimension for long-horizon search than merely increasing reasoning chain length.
A unified agent architecture and data synthesis strategy can enable robust generalization across diverse agentic search tasks, from deterministic question-answering to open-ended research.
originalProblem: Existing deep research agents incur high inference costs and latency due to reliance on scaling sequential reasoning depth and numerous tool calls.
Limited generalization, with agents typically excelling in either deterministic question-answering or open-ended research tasks but struggling to perform across both.
Current synthetic data generation methods lack robust support for creating open-ended research tasks that require flexible information aggregation and cross-source validation.
results: SMTL-100 achieved 43.6% accuracy on the BrowseComp benchmark, reducing the average number of assistant steps by 70.7% (60.4 vs. 206.0) compared to MiroThinker-v1.0-30B.
Demonstrated leading performance across a broad set of heterogeneous benchmarks, including BrowseComp, GAIA, WebWalker, and DeepResearch Bench RACE, confirming robust generalization.
Achieved higher information density per interaction, averaging 3.5 tool calls per step compared to 1.0 for sequential baselines, contributing to enhanced efficiency.
solution: A parallel agentic workflow that decomposes complex tasks into subtasks for concurrent execution and tool coordination, maximizing information density per interaction step.
Dynamic plan refinement and an overflow-triggered context management strategy, including a "plan-centric reset," to handle long-horizon trajectories within fixed context windows.
A unified, high-diversity data construction pipeline using graph networks for hierarchical question generation (Deep Search) and comprehensive research question formulation (Deep Research).
paper_summary.summary: The OPPO AI Agent Team developed "Search More, Think Less" (SMTL), an agentic search framework that employs parallel evidence acquisition and efficient context management. It improves performance on long-horizon tasks while reducing inference steps by 70.7% on BrowseComp and demonstrates robust generalization across both deterministic and open-ended research benchmarks.

title: Search More, Think Less: Rethinking Long-Horizon Agentic Search for Efficiency and Generalization

abstract:
Recent advancements in Large Language Models(LLMs) have demonstrated their
capabilities not only in reasoning but also in invoking external tools,
particularly search engines. However, teaching models to discern when to invoke
search and when to rely on their internal knowledge remains a significant
challenge. Existing reinforcement learning approaches often lead to redundant
search behaviors, resulting in inefficiencies and over-cost. In this paper, we
propose SEM, a novel post-training reinforcement learning framework that
explicitly trains LLMs to optimize search usage. By constructing a balanced
dataset combining MuSiQue and MMLU, we create scenarios where the model must
learn to distinguish between questions it can answer directly and those
requiring external retrieval. We design a structured reasoning template and
employ Group Relative Policy Optimization(GRPO) to post-train the model's
search behaviors. Our reward function encourages accurate answering without
unnecessary search while promoting effective retrieval when needed.
Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly reduces
redundant search operations while maintaining or improving answer accuracy
across multiple challenging benchmarks. This framework advances the model's
reasoning efficiency and extends its capability to judiciously leverage
external knowledge.

keyInsights: Models can be trained to explicitly justify their search decisions through structured reasoning
Relative quality assessment within query groups improves search optimization
Clear distinction between internal knowledge and external search needs improves efficiency
originalProblem: LLMs frequently perform unnecessary searches even when answers are available in their internal knowledge
Existing approaches lack efficient mechanisms to balance between using internal knowledge versus external search tools
results: Achieved higher Exact Match scores on HotpotQA and MuSiQue compared to baseline methods
Significantly reduced search operations on MMLU and GSM8k benchmarks
Demonstrated smoother and more stable learning trajectory compared to existing approaches
Maintained answer accuracy while reducing unnecessary searches by up to 80%
solution: Structured reasoning template with explicit think/answer/search annotations
Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to optimize search behavior
Balanced training dataset combining known and unknown question types
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Ant Group develop SEM, a reinforcement learning framework that teaches large language models to optimize their use of external search tools, reducing redundant searches by up to 80% while maintaining or improving answer accuracy across multiple reasoning benchmarks through structured reasoning templates and group relative policy optimization.

title: SEM: Reinforcement Learning for Search-Efficient Large Language Models

abstract: Recently, large reasoning models have demonstrated strong mathematical and coding abilities, and deep search leverages their reasoning capabilities in challenging information retrieval tasks. Existing deep search works are generally limited to a single knowledge source, either local or the Web. However, enterprises often require private deep search systems that can leverage search tools over both local and the Web corpus. Simply training an agent equipped with multiple search tools using flat reinforcement learning (RL) is a straightforward idea, but it has problems such as low training data efficiency and poor mastery of complex tools. To address the above issue, we propose a hierarchical agentic deep search framework, HierSearch, trained with hierarchical RL. At the low level, a local deep search agent and a Web deep search agent are trained to retrieve evidence from their corresponding domains. At the high level, a planner agent coordinates low-level agents and provides the final answer. Moreover, to prevent direct answer copying and error propagation, we design a knowledge refiner that filters out hallucinations and irrelevant evidence returned by low-level agents. Experiments show that HierSearch achieves better performance compared to flat RL, and outperforms various deep search and multi-source retrieval-augmented generation baselines in six benchmarks across general, finance, and medical domains.

keyInsights: Hierarchical agent architectures, trained with HRL, effectively manage the complexity of multi-tool, multi-knowledge-source deep search by decomposing the problem and enabling specialized learning for subtasks.

Specialized low-level agents, focusing on either local or web knowledge, can achieve superior mastery and utilization of their respective domain-specific search tools, overcoming the limitations of flat RL.

An intelligent knowledge refiner is essential for ensuring robust information exchange in multi-agent systems, preventing the propagation of irrelevant or erroneous data and improving overall system reliability.
originalProblem: Existing deep search systems are limited to a single knowledge source (local or Web) and few tools, which is inadequate for enterprise needs requiring both internal and external information.
Flat reinforcement learning for multi-tool environments suffers from large action spaces, inefficient training, and poor mastery of complex tools like Web search, failing to utilize tool synergy.
Information exchange between agents risks propagating irrelevant search results, hallucinatory reasoning, or direct answer copying, degrading overall system reliability.
results: HierSearch consistently outperformed existing deep search and multi-source RAG baselines across six diverse benchmarks, achieving an F1 score of 62.83 on MuSiQue, surpassing the next best baseline by over 5 F1 points.

Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) demonstrated faster and more stable training, leading to significantly higher Web search success rates (e.g., 81.25% on NQ) compared to the flat RL counterpart (23.50%).

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of both the reasoning-aware knowledge refiner and the dual integration of local and Web agents, with removal of either component leading to substantial performance degradation.
solution: Developed HierSearch, a three-tiered hierarchical agentic framework comprising specialized low-level agents (Local and Web Deep Search) and a high-level Planner Agent for orchestration.
Employed Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) for training, where low-level agents first master domain-specific tools, and the Planner Agent then learns to coordinate these as abstract tools.
Introduced a reasoning-aware knowledge refiner to filter and merge evidence from low-level agents, ensuring only high-quality, relevant information is passed to the planner.
paper_summary.summary: HierSearch presents a hierarchical agentic framework for enterprise deep search, integrating local and web knowledge sources through Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning. The system achieved superior performance, for example, an F1 score of 62.83 on the MuSiQue benchmark, by enabling more stable training and better mastery of complex search tools than non-hierarchical methods.

title: HierSearch: A Hierarchical Enterprise Deep Search Framework Integrating Local and Web Searches

abstract: Recent efforts have augmented language models (LMs) with external tools or environments, leading to the development of language agents that can reason and act. However, most of these agents rely on few-shot prompting techniques with off-the-shelf LMs. In this paper, we investigate and argue for the overlooked direction of fine-tuning LMs to obtain language agents. Using a setup of question answering (QA) with a Google search API, we explore a variety of base LMs, prompting methods, fine-tuning data, and QA tasks, and find language agents are consistently improved after fine-tuning their backbone LMs. For example, fine-tuning Llama2-7B with 500 agent trajectories generated by GPT-4 leads to a 77% HotpotQA performance increase. Furthermore, we propose FireAct, a novel approach to fine-tuning LMs with trajectories from multiple tasks and prompting methods, and show having more diverse fine-tuning data can further improve agents. Along with other findings regarding scaling effects, robustness, generalization, efficiency and cost, our work establishes comprehensive benefits of fine-tuning LMs for agents, and provides an initial set of experimental designs, insights, as well as open questions toward language agent fine-tuning.

keyInsights: Fine-tuning is a highly effective method for developing language agents, offering advantages over few-shot prompting by adapting models directly to agentic behaviors.
Combining diverse prompting methods (ReAct, CoT, Reflexion) in the fine-tuning data significantly enhances agent performance and adaptability, enabling implicit selection of reasoning strategies.

Smaller, fine-tuned open-source models can achieve competitive performance against larger, prompted proprietary models, improving efficiency, accessibility, and reproducibility for language agent development.
originalProblem: Traditional language agents rely heavily on few-shot prompting of large language models (LLMs), which leads to limitations in performance, high computational costs, and challenges with reproducibility.
Pre-trained LLMs are not specifically optimized for complex agentic behaviors like tool use, planning, or self-reflection.
The application of fine-tuning, a common technique for adapting LLMs to specific tasks, remained largely unexplored for enhancing language agent capabilities.
results: Fine-tuning Llama2-7B with 500 GPT-4 generated agent trajectories led to a 77% increase in HotpotQA performance compared to few-shot prompting.
Fine-tuned agents demonstrated improved robustness to noisy tool outputs and better generalization capabilities when tested on unseen tasks like the Bamboogle dataset.
Combining ReAct, Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and Reflexion prompting methods in the fine-tuning data consistently improved agent performance across various language models.
solution: FIREACT (FIne-tuning for REAsonable aCTing) introduces a fine-tuning approach that leverages data diversity by combining trajectories from multiple prompting methods (ReAct, CoT, Reflexion) within the ReAct framework.

The methodology involves generating high-quality agent trajectories using GPT-4, cleaning the data, and then applying Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune smaller language models (e.g., Llama-2, CodeLlama).
This approach enables the fine-tuned agent to implicitly select the most appropriate reasoning and acting strategy based on the task, departing from traditional single-method agents.
paper_summary.summary: FIREACT, developed by Chen et al. from System2 Research and academic institutions, introduces a fine-tuning approach for language agents that leverages diverse trajectories from multiple prompting methods (ReAct, CoT, Reflexion). This method improves agent performance, efficiency, and robustness on question answering tasks, enabling smaller open-source models to achieve competitive results compared to larger prompted models.

title: FireAct: Toward Language Agent Fine-tuning

abstract: Search-augmented LLMs often struggle with complex reasoning tasks due to ineffective multi-hop retrieval and limited reasoning ability. We propose AceSearcher, a cooperative self-play framework that trains a single large language model (LLM) to alternate between two roles: a decomposer that breaks down complex queries and a solver that integrates retrieved contexts for answer generation. AceSearcher couples supervised fine-tuning on a diverse mixture of search, reasoning, and decomposition tasks with reinforcement fine-tuning optimized for final answer accuracy, eliminating the need for intermediate annotations. Extensive experiments on three reasoning-intensive tasks across 10 datasets show that AceSearcher outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an average exact match improvement of 7.6%. Remarkably, on document-level finance reasoning tasks, AceSearcher-32B matches the performance of the DeepSeek-V3 model using less than 5% of its parameters. Even at smaller scales (1.5B and 8B), AceSearcher often surpasses existing search-augmented LLMs with up to 9x more parameters, highlighting its exceptional efficiency and effectiveness in tackling complex reasoning tasks. Our code will be published at this https URL and this https URL.

keyInsights: Training a unified LLM to explicitly handle both question decomposition and answer generation through cooperative self-play significantly enhances its ability to perform multi-step reasoning and search.

An efficient, data-centric two-stage fine-tuning approach, combining comprehensive SFT with iterative preference-based RFT from sparse final rewards, effectively teaches complex reasoning to LLMs without memory-intensive online RL or excessive intermediate supervision.

This methodology enables smaller, open-source LLMs to achieve high parameter efficiency, delivering performance comparable to or surpassing much larger and often proprietary models on challenging multi-hop reasoning tasks.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with complex reasoning and multi-hop information retrieval in search-augmented settings, leading to factual inaccuracies and "hallucinations."
Existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approaches are often limited to simple, single-turn questions or rely on powerful, proprietary LLMs, limiting accessibility and generalizability.
Current methods for complex RAG, such as tree-search or reinforcement learning, often incur high computational costs, memory intensity, or require extensive intermediate human annotations.
results: Achieved an overall exact match (EM) score of 60.7% across 6 multi-hop QA datasets, demonstrating an average improvement of 7.6% over strong state-of-the-art baselines.

Demonstrated exceptional parameter efficiency, with AceSearcher-1.5B matching or exceeding baselines up to 10 times larger, and AceSearcher-8B outperforming existing search-augmented LLMs up to 9 times its size.
Outperformed size-comparable baselines in document-level reasoning tasks by 6.2% (32B model) and 9.0% (8B model), with human evaluations confirming the improved quality of generated subquestions.
solution: A cooperative self-play framework where a single LLM alternates between a "decomposer" role (breaking down complex questions into subquestions) and a "solver" role (integrating retrieved contexts and generating answers).

A two-stage fine-tuning process: Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) on a diverse mixture of context-rich QA, decomposition, and Chain-of-Thought data, followed by Preference-based Reinforcement Fine-tuning (RFT).
RFT leverages multi-turn Direct Preference Optimization (mDPO) with sparse rewards derived solely from the final answer's exact match, minimizing the need for costly intermediate human annotations.
paper_summary.summary: AceSearcher enhances large language models' capabilities for complex reasoning and multi-hop search by introducing a cooperative self-play framework and a two-stage fine-tuning process. The framework, which utilizes Supervised Fine-tuning and preference-based Reinforcement Fine-tuning, outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on multi-hop QA and document-level reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating exceptional parameter efficiency by enabling smaller models to achieve performance comparable to much larger ones.

title: AceSearcher: Bootstrapping Reasoning and Search for LLMs via Reinforced Self-Play

abstract: LLM-based search agents are increasingly trained on entity-centric synthetic data to solve complex, knowledge-intensive tasks. However, prevailing training methods like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) discard this rich entity information, relying instead on sparse, outcome-based rewards. This critical limitation renders them unable to distinguish informative "near-miss" samples-those with substantially correct reasoning but a flawed final answer-from complete failures, thus discarding valuable learning signals. We address this by leveraging the very entities discarded during training. Our empirical analysis reveals a strong positive correlation between the number of ground-truth entities identified during an agent's reasoning process and final answer accuracy. Building on this insight, we introduce Entity-aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (E-GRPO), a novel framework that formulates a dense entity-aware reward function. E-GRPO assigns partial rewards to incorrect samples proportional to their entity match rate, enabling the model to effectively learn from these "near-misses". Experiments on diverse question-answering (QA) and deep research benchmarks show that E-GRPO consistently and significantly outperforms the GRPO baseline. Furthermore, our analysis reveals that E-GRPO not only achieves superior accuracy but also induces more efficient reasoning policies that require fewer tool calls, demonstrating a more effective and sample-efficient approach to aligning search agents.

keyInsights: Ground-truth entities, typically discarded after synthetic data generation, serve as an effective and computationally inexpensive fine-grained proxy for evaluating factual correctness and reasoning quality in agent trajectories.
Providing dense, entity-aware rewards allows search agents to learn efficiently from partially correct "near-miss" reasoning paths, which are otherwise treated as complete failures.
Optimally balancing the weight of entity-matching in the reward function is critical, as excessive emphasis can lead to suboptimal performance.
originalProblem: Prevailing Reinforcement Learning methods for LLM-based search agents, such as GRPO, rely on sparse, binary rewards (correct/incorrect) which discard valuable intermediate reasoning information.
This creates a "near-miss" problem where partially correct agent trajectories, rich in factual accuracy but failing in the final answer, receive no discriminative learning signal.
Existing solutions to reward sparsity, like Process Reward Models, are computationally prohibitive or too expensive to annotate for the open-ended nature of web search tasks.
results: E-GRPO models consistently outperformed GRPO baselines, achieving an average Pass@1 score of 64.2 on local QA benchmarks (2.8 points higher than GRPO) and demonstrated superior performance on challenging deep research benchmarks.
Policies learned by E-GRPO were more efficient, requiring consistently fewer tool calls per rollout compared to GRPO, indicating a more optimized reasoning process.
Training dynamics showed E-GRPO achieved higher and more stable training accuracy, fostering a stronger correlation between entity match rate and final answer accuracy.
solution: Empirically validated that the "entity match rate" (ground-truth entities identified by the agent) strongly correlates with final answer accuracy, confirming its value as a fine-grained proxy for reasoning quality.
Introduced Entity-aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (E-GRPO), an RL framework that repurposes entity information from synthetic data for supervision.

Designed a dense reward function for E-GRPO which assigns partial credit to incorrect rollouts based on their normalized entity match rate, thereby differentiating "near-misses" from complete failures.
paper_summary.summary: ShanghaiTech University and Alibaba Group researchers developed Entity-aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (E-GRPO), which reuses ground-truth entities from synthetic data to provide fine-grained reward signals for training LLM-based search agents. This method improved average Pass@1 scores by 2.8 points over GRPO on QA benchmarks and resulted in agents employing fewer tool calls for more efficient reasoning.

title: Repurposing Synthetic Data for Fine-grained Search Agent Supervision

abstract: Search agents powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in tackling knowledge-intensive tasks. Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for training these agents to perform complex, multi-step reasoning. However, prior RL-based methods often rely on sparse or rule-based rewards, which can lead agents to commit to suboptimal or erroneous reasoning paths without the ability to recover. To address these limitations, we propose ReSeek, a novel self-correcting framework for training search agents. Our framework introduces a self-correction mechanism that empowers the agent to dynamically identify and recover from erroneous search paths during an episode. By invoking a special JUDGE action, the agent can judge the information and re-plan its search strategy. To guide this process, we design a dense, instructive process reward function, which decomposes into a correctness reward for retrieving factual information and a utility reward for finding information genuinely useful for the query. Furthermore, to mitigate the risk of data contamination in existing datasets, we introduce FictionalHot, a new and challenging benchmark with recently curated questions requiring complex reasoning. Being intuitively reasonable and practically simple, extensive experiments show that agents trained with ReSeek significantly outperform SOTA baselines in task success rate and path faithfulness.

keyInsights: Dynamic self-correction through a dedicated 'JUDGE' action allows LLM agents to effectively filter unhelpful information and re-plan, moving beyond rigid linear reasoning.
Dense, intermediate reward signals, grounded in objective utility, are crucial for training agents to develop robust self-assessment and error recovery skills in complex search tasks.

Contamination-resistant benchmarks, like FictionalHot, are essential for accurately evaluating an agent's genuine reasoning abilities by isolating them from reliance on memorized pre-trained knowledge.
originalProblem: Current Reinforcement Learning (RL) search agents struggle to recover from early reasoning errors, often committing to suboptimal paths due to sparse or heuristic reward functions.
Existing reward structures in RL-based search agents provide insufficient or delayed guidance for complex, multi-step decision-making.
Evaluation benchmarks for knowledge-intensive tasks often suffer from data contamination, conflating an agent's genuine reasoning ability with its memorized pre-training knowledge.
results: ReSeek achieved an average Exact Match (EM) score of 0.377 with a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model, outperforming the next best baseline (ZeroSearch at 0.346) on eight open-domain QA benchmarks.
The 'JUDGE' action demonstrated positive outcomes, providing beneficial interventions in 40-50% of cases across all benchmark settings, validating its reliability in self-correction.
On the FictionalHot benchmark, ReSeek's performance exhibited minimal differences between model sizes, confirming its effectiveness in isolating genuine reasoning ability from pre-trained knowledge.
solution: Introduced ReSeek, a self-correcting framework incorporating a 'JUDGE' action, enabling agents to dynamically evaluate retrieved information and adapt their search strategy mid-episode.

Developed a dense, instructive reward function ($R_{judge}$) that provides fine-grained feedback on the factual correctness and contextual utility of information, aligning agent judgments with objective task progress.
Created the FictionalHot benchmark, a new closed-world dataset using fictional entities to mitigate data contamination and rigorously assess procedural search and reasoning capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: The ReSeek framework from Tencent and Tsinghua University implements a self-correcting mechanism for LLM-powered search agents, employing a novel JUDGE action and dense, instructive rewards. This approach enhances accuracy on knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks and improves multi-step reasoning capabilities, validated against a new contamination-resistant FictionalHot benchmark.

title: ReSeek: A Self-Correcting Framework for Search Agents with Instructive Rewards

abstract: Search agents have emerged as a pivotal paradigm for solving open-ended, knowledge-intensive reasoning tasks. However, training these agents via Reinforcement Learning (RL) faces a critical dilemma: interacting with live commercial Web APIs is prohibitively expensive, while relying on static data snapshots often introduces noise due to data misalignment. This misalignment generates corrupted reward signals that destabilize training by penalizing correct reasoning or rewarding hallucination. To address this, we propose SearchGym, a simulation environment designed to bootstrap robust search agents. SearchGym employs a rigorous generative pipeline to construct a verifiable knowledge graph and an aligned document corpus, ensuring that every reasoning task is factually grounded and strictly solvable. Building on this controllable environment, we introduce SearchGym-RL, a curriculum learning methodology that progressively optimizes agent policies through purified feedback, evolving from basic interactions to complex, long-horizon planning. Extensive experiments across the Llama and Qwen families demonstrate strong Sim-to-Real generalization. Notably, our Qwen2.5-7B-Base model trained within SearchGym surpasses the web-enhanced ASearcher baseline across nine diverse benchmarks by an average relative margin of 10.6%. Our results validate that high-fidelity simulation serves as a scalable and highly cost-effective methodology for developing capable search agents.

keyInsights: Providing purified, verifiable feedback signals is crucial for stable and effective Reinforcement Learning in complex, knowledge-intensive tasks, mitigating the instability caused by noisy data.
A dual `Search` and `Access` action space is essential for compelling agents to develop rigorous information evaluation and synthesis skills, as opposed to simpler action sets.

Hierarchical skill acquisition through curriculum learning is highly effective for training agents on tasks with sparse rewards and long reasoning horizons, allowing progressive mastery of complex behaviors.
originalProblem: Training autonomous search agents with LLMs using Reinforcement Learning is constrained by the high financial cost of interacting with live commercial web APIs.
Relying on static, offline datasets for training introduces significant data misalignment and noise, leading to corrupted reward signals and unstable policy optimization.
Existing simulation environments are often insufficient for training agents on complex, long-horizon reasoning tasks due to inaccuracies or limited scope.
results: SearchGym-RL agents consistently surpassed all baseline approaches, including RAG and other simulated environments, on diverse QA benchmarks, achieving an average relative improvement of 10.4% over Search-R1.

Agents trained solely in SearchGym demonstrated strong sim-to-real generalization, outperforming web-trained baselines (e.g., ASearcher) on real-world tasks like GAIA (3.89% absolute improvement) and xbench-DeepSearch (17.00% absolute improvement).

The data generation process for SearchGym is highly cost-effective, incurring only $50 for over 41,000 verified QA pairs, eliminating the prohibitive financial barrier associated with live web API training.
solution: SearchGym constructs a synthetic world model comprising a structured knowledge graph and an associated searchable document corpus, generated by an LLM based on predefined schemas.
An 'edge verifiability' mechanism ensures that all reasoning paths within the synthesized knowledge graph are discoverable via search actions, providing clean and learnable ground truth.

The SearchGym-RL framework employs a two-stage curriculum learning approach to optimize agent policies, progressing from foundational skill acquisition on simple tasks to advanced reasoning development on complex, multi-hop problems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from HKUST, HKU, and CUHK introduced SearchGym, a cost-effective and high-fidelity simulation environment for training autonomous search agents. It generates verifiable knowledge graphs and an aligned document corpus to provide clean, noise-free reward signals for reinforcement learning. Agents trained in SearchGym achieved superior performance and robust sim-to-real generalization on real-world QA benchmarks, with data generation costing only $50.

title: SearchGym: Bootstrapping Real-World Search Agents via Cost-Effective and High-Fidelity Environment Simulation

abstract: Reasoning-augmented search agents, such as Search-R1, are trained to reason, search, and generate the final answer iteratively. Nevertheless, due to their limited capabilities in reasoning and search, their performance on multi-hop QA benchmarks remains far from satisfactory. To handle complex or compound queries, we train an LLM-based search agent with the native capability of query expansion through reinforcement learning. In each turn, our search agent proposes several query variants, which are searched simultaneously to cover more relevant information. Meanwhile, given limited post-training data and computing resources, it is very challenging for a search agent to master multiple tasks, including query generation, retrieved information understanding, and answer generation. Therefore, we propose incorporating a pre-trained squeezer model that helps the search agent understand the retrieved documents, allowing the search agent to focus on query generation for high retrieval recall. With the assistance of the squeezer model, we discover that even a small-scale 3B LLM can demonstrate a strong capability of query expansion and achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on the multi-hop QA benchmarks. To be specific, our experiments across seven question-answering benchmarks demonstrate that our method, named ExpandSearch, achieves an average improvement of 4.4% compared to state-of-the-art baselines, with strong gains on multi-hop reasoning tasks requiring diverse evidence aggregation.

keyInsights: Query expansion significantly improves recall by generating diverse query variants, effectively capturing multi-faceted information for complex questions.
Selective information distillation via a "squeezer" is essential to combat information overload, filtering noise and providing the LLM with a high-signal context for reasoning.
Reinforcement learning is crucial for teaching LLMs to autonomously generate effective query expansions and coordinate the "expand-then-squeeze" process.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based search agents often generate semantically narrow, single queries, limiting the recall of diverse information for complex or multi-hop questions.
Retrieving extensive content leads to information overload, where irrelevant facts obscure crucial evidence and degrade the LLM's reasoning ability, a phenomenon known as "lost in the middle."
results: ExpandSearch achieved an average of 4.4% absolute improvement in Exact Match (EM) scores over state-of-the-art baselines across seven diverse QA benchmarks.
A 3B-Instruct ExpandSearch model achieved an average EM score of 0.457, outperforming 7B baseline methods and matching the performance of state-of-the-art 7B models.
The framework demonstrated robust generalization, showing a 5.2% average EM improvement on out-of-domain benchmarks, and both syntax and semantic query expansions proved essential for performance.
solution: ExpandSearch introduces an "expand-then-squeeze" strategy, wherein the main LLM generates multiple diverse query variants in parallel.
A pre-trained, frozen "squeezer" LLM then distills all retrieved results into a compact, reasoning-relevant summary to mitigate information overload.
The search agent is trained using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with verifiable exact match and format rewards to learn effective expansion and search strategies.
paper_summary.summary: The ExpandSearch framework, developed at NVIDIA, enhances Large Language Model search agents by employing an "expand-then-squeeze" strategy to overcome single-query limitations and information overload. This approach consistently improves Exact Match scores by an average of 4.4% across seven QA benchmarks, enabling 3B models to surpass larger 7B baselines.

title: Beyond the limitation of a single query: Train your LLM for query expansion with Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Web agents powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for next-generation AI, but their limited reasoning in uncertain, dynamic web environments hinders robust deployment. In this paper, we identify key reasoning skills essential for effective web agents, i.e., reflection & lookahead, branching, and rollback, and curate trajectory data that exemplifies these abilities by reconstructing the agent's (inference-time) reasoning algorithms into chain-of-thought rationales. We conduct experiments in the agent self-improving benchmark, OpenWebVoyager, and demonstrate that distilling salient reasoning patterns into the backbone LLM via simple fine-tuning can substantially enhance its performance. Our approach yields significant improvements across multiple benchmarks, including WebVoyager, Mind2web-live, and SimpleQA (web search), highlighting the potential of targeted reasoning skill enhancement for web agents.

keyInsights: Explicitly defining and distilling web-specific reasoning patterns (reflection, branching, rollback) through SFT significantly enhances web agent performance beyond generic LLM reasoning or costly RL.
Verbalizing complex reasoning processes into refined, human-like Chain-of-Thought rationales is more effective for SFT than relying on the agent's original self-generated thoughts.

A cumulative training strategy that introduces more complex reasoning only for tasks that failed at simpler stages prevents 'overthinking' and improves the agent's overall learning effectiveness and efficiency.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM)-powered web agents exhibit limited reasoning capabilities, struggling with uncertainties and dynamic web environments.
Generic Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) often 'overthink' and produce inefficient solutions for specialized web agent tasks.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches for web agents suffer from unpredictable reasoning, high interaction costs, and difficulty adapting to the stochasticity of open-domain web environments.
results: WEBCOT achieves 41.04% accuracy on WebVoyager (66.8% relative improvement over LLAMA-3.3-70B baseline) and 20.8% on Mind2Web-Live (264.9% relative improvement), outperforming GPT-4O on WebVoyager.

Ablation studies demonstrate the cumulative benefit of all three reasoning components, with reflection, branching, and rollback successively improving performance, reaching 41.04% on WebVoyager with the full framework.

The rollback mechanism reduces trajectory length (mean ∆L of -0.23, median -1.0 for successful tasks) for greater efficiency, and WEBCOT exhibits higher token efficiency (422.2 tokens/query) compared to WebDreamer (2665.6 tokens/query).
solution: The WEBCOT framework employs a self-improvement optimization and Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) strategy to distill web-specific reasoning patterns into a backbone LLM (LLAMA-3.3-70B).
It reconstructs crucial reasoning skills—reflection & lookahead, branching, and rollback—into refined Chain-of-Thought (CoT) rationales from curated trajectory data.
A cumulative training strategy sequentially adds these advanced reasoning patterns, starting with basic successful trajectories, to prevent 'overthinking' and optimize learning.
paper_summary.summary: WEBCOT, developed by Tencent AI Lab and CUHK, enhances web agent reasoning in Large Language Models by reconstructing and distilling specific human-like reasoning patterns—reflection, branching, and rollback—via supervised fine-tuning. This framework achieves up to a 264.9% relative accuracy improvement on web navigation benchmarks compared to baseline models and surpasses GPT-4O on WebVoyager and SimpleQA.

title: WebCoT: Enhancing Web Agent Reasoning by Reconstructing Chain-of-Thought in Reflection, Branching, and Rollback

abstract: Long-horizon agentic search requires iteratively exploring the web over long trajectories and synthesizing information across many sources, and is the foundation for enabling powerful applications like deep research systems.
